steps.title: Neonatology
steps.text: Multiple Choice for Secondary Care

steps.title: Neonatal resuscitation
steps.text: What key conditions must be managed during neonatal care at the secondary level?
steps.rationale: At the secondary level, management includes stabilizing oxygenation, glucose levels, and fluid balance to prevent further complications in at-risk newborns.

steps.title: Neonatal resuscitation
steps.text: What is the appropriate feeding method for a neonate with poor suck and reduced gag reflex?
steps.rationale: NG feeding is recommended in such cases to reduce the high risk of milk aspiration.

steps.title: Neonatal resuscitation
steps.text: When should a full septic screen be performed in a neonate with temperature instability?
steps.rationale: Persistent temperature >38°C may indicate infection and requires a full septic workup.

steps.title: Neonatal resuscitation
steps.text: When should a newborn be referred from secondary to tertiary care?
steps.rationale: Referral is required for moderate to severe hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy (HIE), or if the baby has persistent seizures, cardiorespiratory or metabolic instability.

textChoices.text: If moderate–severe HIE or persistent instability

steps.title: CARE OF PREMATURE, SMALL FOR GESTATIONAL AGE AND LOW BIRTH WEIGHT
steps.text: What is the correct management for an unstable preterm baby at a secondary or tertiary care facility?
steps.rationale: All unstable preterm babies must be stabilized and referred appropriately. Delaying referral or initiating KMC before stabilization is not safe.

textChoices.text: Stabilize the baby and refer to a higher-level facility if needed
Start oral feeds and discharge if vitals normalize
Observe for 24 hours and reassess later
Begin KMC immediately without further action

steps.title: CARE OF PREMATURE, SMALL FOR GESTATIONAL AGE AND LOW BIRTH WEIGHT
steps.text: Which of the following stable preterm babies should be referred to tertiary care for specialized KMC?
steps.rationale: According to the guideline, stable babies with birth weight <1500g (like 1400g) must be referred to tertiary care. Babies between 1500g–2000g can be managed at the secondary level in a KMC ward.

steps.title: SMALL FOR GESTATIONAL AGE
steps.text: What is the recommended feeding approach for an SGA baby at secondary or tertiary care level?
steps.rationale: Early feeding using NG, cup, or breast is essential for metabolic stability in SGA babies.

textChoices.text: Delay feeding for 12 hours to avoid aspiration
Start IV fluids before any oral feeding attempt
Initiate feeding as early as possible using NG tube, cup, or breast
Withhold feeds until weight reaches 2.5 kg

steps.title: SMALL FOR GESTATIONAL AGE
steps.text: What should be done if an SGA baby is too sick to tolerate oral feeds?
steps.rationale: The guideline allows IV fluids when the baby is too sick to tolerate oral feeds.

textChoices.text: Continue trying cup feeds every 2 hours
Consider starting IV fluids for stabilization
Wait until the baby cries before feeding

steps.title: SMALL FOR GESTATIONAL AGE
steps.text: Which of the following interventions is appropriate for an SGA baby who is symptomatic or has sepsis risk factors?
steps.rationale: Babies with symptoms or risk factors for infection should be managed per the sepsis protocol and provided thermal protection, such as drying, hat, IKMC, or heater.

textChoices.text: Delay antibiotics until blood cultures are positive
Refer directly to tertiary without any care
Warm the baby only if temperature falls below 35°C
Consider antibiotics and provide warmth using IKMC, heater, hat, or drying

steps.title: SMALL FOR GESTATIONAL AGE
steps.text: When should antibiotics be considered for a Small for Gestational Age (SGA) baby at the secondary or tertiary level?
steps.rationale: According to the guideline, antibiotics should be considered if the baby is symptomatic or has sepsis risk factors, following the sepsis protocol. Early intervention can be life-saving even before test results are available.

textChoices.text: If the baby shows symptoms or has risk factors for sepsis
For all SGA babies regardless of symptoms
Only when CRP and blood culture results are positive

steps.title: KANGAROO MOTHER CARE (KMC/ (IKMC))
steps.text: What is the correct action for an unstable preterm baby at a secondary care facility?
steps.rationale: Unstable preterm babies must be stabilized and referred to tertiary care for advanced support, even if KMC is not immediately initiated.

textChoices.text: Admit to KMC ward for 48-hour observation
Wait until the baby’s weight improves before acting
Refer the baby to a tertiary care facility after stabilization

steps.title: KANGAROO MOTHER CARE (KMC/ (IKMC))
steps.text: How should a stable preterm baby weighing 1800g be managed at a secondary facility?
steps.rationale: Stable babies weighing 1500g–2000g should be admitted to the KMC ward at the secondary level for continued care, feeding, and warmth.

textChoices.text: Admitted to the KMC ward at the secondary level
Discharged with home KMC instructions only
Kept in an incubator without KMC

steps.title: NEONATAL HYPOGLYCAEMIA
steps.text: A newborn's RBS remains <2.5 mmol/L after 30 minutes of the first glucose bolus. What is the next best step?
steps.rationale: If RBS is still low after 30 minutes, repeat the same emergency treatment (IV/PO dextrose or glucose gel).

steps.title: NEONATAL HYPOGLYCAEMIA
steps.text: If a baby’s RBS remains <2.5 mmol/L after two glucose boluses, what is the correct next step?
steps.rationale: After 2 boluses, initiate a 10% dextrose infusion, begin feeding, and monitor RBS every 2 hours.

textChoices.text: Start IV infusion with 10% dextrose and optimize feeds

steps.title: NEONATAL HYPOGLYCAEMIA
steps.text: If a baby is receiving 100 ml/kg/day of 10% dextrose, what is the glucose infusion rate (GIR)?
steps.rationale: GIR = 100 x 10 / (10 x 0.07) = 7.0 mg/kg/min. This is calculated to monitor glucose delivery and detect possible hyperinsulinaemia if GIR > 10.

steps.title: NEONATAL HYPOGLYCAEMIA
steps.text: When should a newborn with hypoglycemia be referred to tertiary care from a secondary facility?
steps.rationale: The baby should be referred to tertiary care if hypoglycemia persists after appropriate bolus and IV dextrose treatment, as it may indicate an underlying issue like hyperinsulinaemia.

steps.title: NEONATAL HYPERGLYCAEMIA
steps.text: What is the most appropriate immediate response when hyperglycaemia is identified in a neonate receiving IV fluids?
steps.rationale: The first step in managing neonatal hyperglycaemia is to stop any ongoing glucose infusion to prevent worsening of hyperglycaemia.

textChoices.text: Add insulin to the current fluid

steps.title: NEONATAL HYPERGLYCAEMIA
steps.text: Which maintenance fluid is appropriate for a neonate with hyperglycaemia after stopping glucose-containing fluids?
steps.rationale: After stopping glucose, initiate glucose-free maintenance fluids, such as normal saline or Ringer’s Lactate, to manage hydration safely.

steps.title: NEONATAL HYPERGLYCAEMIA
steps.text: How often should blood glucose be monitored in a neonate under treatment for hyperglycaemia?
steps.rationale: Frequent monitoring—every 2 hours—is essential to assess treatment response and avoid complications.

steps.title: NEONATAL HYPERGLYCAEMIA
steps.text: Under which condition should a hyperglycaemic neonate be referred to tertiary care?
steps.rationale: Referral is necessary if hyperglycaemia lasts beyond 6 hours or the baby is clinically sick, indicating the need for specialized investigation and care.

textChoices.text: If glucose levels normalize within 2 hours
If the baby feeds well but has low-grade fever
If hyperglycaemia persists beyond 6 hours or the baby is clinically unwell

steps.title: NEONATAL SEPSIS
steps.text: A stable newborn in the first 72 hours of life should be screened for sepsis if:
steps.rationale: According to the guideline, maternal infection or infection in the twin is a clear risk factor warranting sepsis screening.

textChoices.text: The baby has poor feeding without other symptoms
There is evidence of suspected or confirmed maternal infection

steps.title: NEONATAL SEPSIS
steps.text: What is the standard sepsis screening protocol in a secondary-level facility for newborns within the first 72 hours?
steps.rationale: The recommended screen includes blood, CSF, and urine cultures to evaluate for neonatal sepsis.

textChoices.text: Blood culture, CSF analysis, and urine culture

steps.title: NEONATAL SEPSIS
steps.text: In a term baby with respiratory distress requiring CPAP within the first 72 hours, what is the appropriate course of action?
steps.rationale: The use of CPAP in a term baby is a red flag. Guidelines advise screening and starting antibiotics immediately.

textChoices.text: Start antibiotics and screen for sepsis
Refer directly to tertiary care without screening

steps.title: NEONATAL SEPSIS
steps.text: Which of the following is an appropriate reason to initiate antibiotics in a newborn at the secondary care level?
steps.rationale: Convulsions in the first 72 hours are serious and are an indication for sepsis evaluation and treatment.

textChoices.text: The baby was small for gestational age
The baby has convulsions within the first 72 hours

steps.title: NEONATAL SEPSIS
steps.text: When should a baby with suspected sepsis be referred from secondary to tertiary care?
steps.rationale: Referral to tertiary care is required if the newborn’s condition deteriorates or does not respond to treatment.

textChoices.text: If birth weight is less than 2.5 kg
If sepsis is suspected and the baby is deteriorating or not improving

steps.title: NEONATAL SEPSIS
steps.text: A baby with suspected meningitis and unstable vitals is being managed in a secondary care facility. What is the most appropriate action?
steps.rationale: Suspected meningitis with instability requires urgent antibiotics and referral to tertiary care without delay.

textChoices.text: Delay antibiotics until CSF results return
Start antibiotics only if fever develops

steps.title: APNOEA OF PREMATURITY
steps.text: What is the preferred respiratory stimulant for a preterm baby <34 weeks gestation presenting with apnoea at the secondary level?
steps.rationale: Aminophylline is the preferred respiratory stimulant for babies <34 weeks, with a loading dose of 6 mg/kg followed by maintenance 2.5 mg/kg BD.

steps.title: APNOEA OF PREMATURITY
steps.text: Which combination of interventions is most appropriate for monitoring and support of a baby with apnoea of prematurity at secondary level?
steps.rationale: Management includes continuous monitoring via pulse oximetry and cardiac leads, and CPAP to support breathing.

textChoices.text: Oxygen hood and daily glucose monitoring
Pulse oximetry, cardiac monitoring, and CPAP

steps.title: APNOEA OF PREMATURITY
steps.text: Which statement is correct about caffeine use in the treatment of apnoea of prematurity at secondary level?
steps.rationale: The standard maintenance dose of caffeine is 2.5 mg/kg/dose, which can be increased to 5 mg/kg/dose if needed. Higher doses require consultant approval.

textChoices.text: Caffeine should only be given if baby is <28 weeks
Maintenance dose starts at 2.5 mg/kg and may be increased up to 5 mg/kg/dose
Caffeine is contraindicated if aminophylline was previously used
A fixed maintenance dose of 10 mg/kg/day must always be used

steps.title: APNOEA OF PREMATURITY
steps.text: Which of the following warrants referral of a preterm baby with apnoea to tertiary care?
steps.rationale: Babies with recurrent apnoea or instability should be referred to tertiary care for further evaluation and specialized management.

textChoices.text: Stable baby with resolved single apnoea episode
Term baby with mild feeding difficulty
Recurrent apnoea episodes or clinical instability

steps.title: NEONATAL JAUNDICE
steps.text: According to clinical grading of jaundice, involvement of the lower abdomen (yellow) corresponds to what serum bilirubin level?
steps.rationale: The chart defines lower abdomen involvement as grade 3 jaundice, which corresponds to serum bilirubin levels of 12–14 mg/dL.

steps.title: NEONATAL JAUNDICE
steps.text: When should phototherapy be initiated for a healthy term baby on Day 3 of life?
steps.rationale: According to the threshold chart, phototherapy is started at 18 mg/dL for healthy term babies on Day 3.

steps.title: NEONATAL JAUNDICE
steps.text: During phototherapy, how often should the baby ideally be repositioned to ensure full body exposure?
steps.rationale: The baby should ideally be repositioned every 2–3 hours to ensure the whole body receives exposure to the therapeutic blue light.

steps.title: NEONATAL JAUNDICE
steps.text: A preterm baby (Day 2 of life, <2000g) presents with a serum bilirubin level of 16 mg/dL. What is the correct management?
steps.rationale: Initiate phototherapy

textChoices.text: No treatment needed unless baby is visibly jaundiced

steps.title: NEONATAL JAUNDICE
steps.text: What is a key indicator that phototherapy is effective in reducing bilirubin levels?
steps.rationale: A successful phototherapy session should reduce bilirubin levels by at least 1 mg/dL every 6 hours.

textChoices.text: Bilirubin dropping by 0.5 mg/dL every 6 hours
No further spread of jaundice to the limbs
Bilirubin decreasing by ≥1 mg/dL every 6 hours

steps.title: NEONATAL JAUNDICE
steps.text: Which of the following is a known complication of phototherapy?
steps.rationale: Retinal damage is a documented complication of phototherapy, which is why babies’ eyes must be shielded during treatment.

steps.title: NEONATAL JAUNDICE
steps.text: According to guidelines, when should phototherapy be stopped?
steps.rationale: Phototherapy is discontinued when bilirubin falls more than 3 mg/dL below the treatment threshold value.

textChoices.text: When bilirubin has dropped below the starting threshold
When bilirubin is more than 3 mg/dL below treatment threshold
After jaundice fades visibly from the face

steps.title: NEONATAL JAUNDICE
steps.text: A 36-hour-old baby on phototherapy is not improving, and total serum bilirubin has reached 22 mg/dL. What should be the immediate step?
steps.rationale: Prepare for exchange transfusion and refer to tertiary care

textChoices.text: Prepare for exchange transfusion and refer to tertiary care
Discontinue feeds to reduce bilirubin production

steps.title: PROLONGED JAUNDICE
steps.text: What is the appropriate management of a neonate with prolonged jaundice at the primary or secondary care level?
steps.rationale: Prolonged jaundice (lasting beyond 14 days in term or 21 days in preterm infants) requires evaluation at the tertiary level to investigate causes such as biliary atresia, hypothyroidism, or infections. Early referral is essential to avoid delays in diagnosis and management.

textChoices.text: Prescribe multivitamins and observe at home
Refer the baby to tertiary level for further evaluation
Initiate paracetamol and monitor for 48 hours

steps.title: NEONATAL SEIZURES
steps.text: At what point should phenobarbitone be administered to a neonate with seizures at the secondary level?
steps.rationale: According to the guideline, phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg IM stat should be given if seizures are frequent (>2/hour) or prolonged (>3 min).

textChoices.text: Only if seizures occur for more than 10 minutes continuously
If seizures occur more than twice per hour or last longer than 3 minutes
Only after a CT scan confirms the cause

steps.title: NEONATAL SEIZURES
steps.text: Which neonate should be referred to tertiary care from a secondary facility?
steps.rationale: Referral is indicated for persistent seizures or if there is a known underlying pathology, both of which require advanced diagnostic and specialist care.

textChoices.text: A baby with one brief seizure that resolved
A baby with persistent seizures or an identified underlying cause
A baby who vomited after feeding
A baby with jitteriness when handled
